title: The Norman Conquest, 1066
subject: history
courseDescription: The succession crisis of 1066, the Battle of Hastings and how the Normans took control of England.
lesson:
The Norman Conquest, 1066

Key facts
Level: GCSE
Theme: British History
Period: 1066 — c.1087

Key question. How did William of Normandy win the English throne in 1066 and hold on to it?

When Edward the Confessor died childless in 1066, three men claimed the throne. After defeating a Viking invasion, Harold Godwinson lost to William of Normandy at the Battle of Hastings. William then secured control through castles, the feudal system and the Domesday Book.

1 · The succession crisis

Edward left no heir, so rivals competed for the crown: Harold Godwinson (crowned in England), William of Normandy (claiming a promise and an oath) and the Viking Harald Hardrada.

Term · Claimant
Someone who says they have the right to the throne. In 1066 each claimant used a different argument — blood, a promise, or an oath — to justify his claim.

2 · Two invasions, two battles

In September, Harold marched north and destroyed the Vikings at the Battle of Stamford Bridge. Days later, William landed in the south. Harold rushed back with a tired army and was defeated and killed at the Battle of Hastings on 14 October 1066.

3 · Taking control

William faced rebellions, especially in the north. He crushed them with the brutal Harrying of the North (1069-70). He secured England by building castles, imposing the feudal system, and ordering the Domesday Book (1086) to record and tax the land.

In summary

Timeline of 1066

DateEvent

TermMeaning
ClaimantSomeone claiming the throne

Motte-and-baileyEarly earth-and-timber castle
Domesday BookSurvey of English land (1086)

People

NameRole
Edward the ConfessorKing who died childless in 1066
Harold GodwinsonEnglish king, killed at Hastings
William of NormandyWinner; became William I
Harald HardradaViking claimant, beaten in the north
Watch out
Harold Godwinson did not survive Hastings — he was killed there (traditionally by an arrow to the eye). And remember the two battles are different: Stamford Bridge (vs the Vikings, north) came before Hastings (vs William, south).

1066 is the most famous date in English history. In a single year, a Norman duke overturned the English elite — reshaping the country's rulers, its land, its laws and even its language for centuries to come.

cards.recto: Which English king died in January 1066, triggering a succession crisis?
cards.explanation: Edward died childless, leaving no clear heir. This sparked a struggle for the throne between several rival claimants.

cards.recto: Who was crowned King of England immediately after Edward the Confessor's death?
cards.explanation: The most powerful English earl, Harold was crowned in January 1066. He claimed Edward had promised him the throne on his deathbed.

cards.recto: Which Duke of Normandy claimed that Edward had promised him the English throne?
cards.explanation: William claimed Edward had named him heir and that Harold had sworn an oath to support him. He invaded to enforce his claim.

cards.recto: Which Norwegian king also invaded England in 1066, claiming the throne?
cards.explanation: The Viking king Harald Hardrada invaded the north with support from Harold's exiled brother Tostig. He was defeated before William landed.

cards.recto: At which battle did Harold Godwinson defeat the Viking invaders in September 1066?
cards.explanation: Harold marched north and crushed Harald Hardrada near York. But this left his army tired and far from the south coast.

cards.recto: [Compare] Why was the timing of the two invasions of 1066 so damaging for Harold?
cards.verso: He beat the Vikings in the north, then had to rush south to face William with a tired army
cards.explanation: Harold won at Stamford Bridge (north) but William landed in the south days later. Harold's exhausted, depleted army then had to march back down to Hastings.

easy: He faced both enemies at the same place on the same day
medium: He fought William first, then the Vikings
Both invasions landed in the north together
He had a fresh, rested army at Hastings
hard: He defeated William in the north before Hastings
Both armies allied against him at once
The Vikings landed in the south, William in the north

cards.recto: At which battle did William defeat Harold on 14 October 1066?
cards.explanation: William's cavalry and archers overcame Harold's shield wall. Harold was killed, and William won control of England.

cards.recto: [Watch out] How did Harold Godwinson die at the Battle of Hastings, according to tradition?
cards.verso: He was killed (traditionally, by an arrow to the eye)
cards.explanation: A common myth is that Harold survived. In fact he was killed at Hastings; the Bayeux Tapestry is often read as showing an arrow in his eye, ending English resistance.

easy: He survived and fled to Normandy
medium: He died of illness after the battle
hard: He won the battle and reigned for years
He died of old age in 1087

cards.recto: What woven artwork tells the story of the Norman Conquest?
cards.explanation: This embroidered cloth, over 68 metres long, depicts events leading to Hastings. It is a key source, though made from the Norman point of view.

cards.recto: What fortifications did the Normans build across England to control the population?
cards.explanation: Motte-and-bailey castles were quick to build from earth and timber. They let a small Norman elite dominate and defend conquered territory.

cards.recto: What was the great survey of England ordered by William in 1085-1086?
cards.explanation: It recorded who owned land and how much it was worth, so William could tax his kingdom and confirm Norman control of English land.

cards.recto: What was the harsh campaign of 1069-1070 to crush rebellion in northern England called?
cards.explanation: William devastated the north, destroying villages, crops and livestock. Thousands died of starvation, ending resistance through terror.

cards.recto: What land system did the Normans use, granting land in return for military service?
cards.explanation: William gave land to barons, who owed him knights and loyalty; barons granted land to knights, and so on. It bound society together under the king.

cards.recto: At the top of the feudal system, who owned all the land in England?
cards.explanation: In theory the king owned all land and granted it out in exchange for service and loyalty. This gave the Norman monarchy great power.

cards.recto: Which language did the new Norman ruling class speak, influencing English for centuries?
cards.explanation: French became the language of the court, law and nobility. Its blend with Old English shaped modern English vocabulary.

cards.recto: Overall, what was the main long-term result of the Norman Conquest?
cards.verso: A new Norman ruling class replaced the English elite and transformed land, law and language
cards.explanation: Within 20 years almost all English lords were replaced by Normans. The Conquest reshaped landholding, the Church, government and the English language.

medium: The English kept all their land and power
France and England merged into one country
hard: The Anglo-Saxon elite stayed in charge
English replaced French at court immediately
